doc.title: Lessor Sales-Type Lease — Commencement
doc.event: Hale Equipment (a dealer) leases a machine to a customer: 4 annual payments of $50,000 due at the beginning of each year, 8% implicit rate, PV of payments $178,855 (equal to the machine's fair value). Collectibility is probable. Prepare Hale's entries at commencement, including receipt of the first payment.

entries.label: Record the sale — receivable at PV, gross profit $48,855

lines.why: Receivable = PV of the lease payments, not the gross 200,000.
lines.trap: Booking the gross payments overstates the receivable — the difference is unearned interest.

lines.why: Sales price = the PV. A sales-type lease is a sale in substance.
The machine's carrying value to the dealer.
The asset leaves Hale's books — it's been 'sold' via lease.

entries.label: Receive the first payment (due at commencement)

lines.why: All principal — no time has passed, so no interest yet.

entries.label: Record the sale — cost equals fair value, gross profit $0
Match the cost — no profit spread

lines.why: Cost = fair value, so COGS = Sales and gross profit is zero. Income comes only from interest over the term.

memorize.body: Lessor gross profit = PV of lease payments (sales price) − cost of the asset
Sales-type = the lease meets ANY finance-lease criterion from the lessor's side. Selling profit exists only when fair value > cost.

questions.prompt: PV of payments $178,855, gross payments $200,000, cost $130,000. Gross profit at commencement = ?

distractors.feedback: You used the gross 200,000 as the sales price. Sales = PV of payments (178,855); the extra 21,145 is future interest, not profit today.
With collectibility probable, a sales-type lease recognizes the selling profit AT commencement — no deferral.

build.scaffold: Three entries: (1) receivable vs sales at PV, (2) COGS vs inventory at cost, (3) day-one cash receipt — all principal.
